concept_name: Moment of Inertia for Different Shapes
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Moment of inertia I = Σmᵢrᵢ² (discrete) or ∫r²dm (continuous)
Thin ring about axis through centre: I = MR²
Solid disc/cylinder about axis through centre: I = ½MR²
Solid sphere about diameter: I = ⅖MR²
Thin rod about centre: I = ML²/12; about end: I = ML²/3
Hollow sphere about diameter: I = ⅔MR²
Parallel axis theorem: I = I_cm + Md²
Perpendicular axis theorem (planar bodies): I_z = I_x + I_y
truth_statements: The moment of inertia depends on the mass distribution relative to the axis of rotation, not just the total mass
A ring has larger moment of inertia than a disc of same mass and radius because all mass is at maximum distance from axis
The parallel axis theorem allows calculating I about any axis parallel to one through the centre of mass
The perpendicular axis theorem applies only to planar (2D) bodies
Moment of inertia is the rotational analogue of mass — it determines resistance to angular acceleration

aha_moment.what_to_show: Ring, disc, and solid sphere of same M and R shown side by side with mass distribution highlighted. Ring: all mass at R (I = MR²). Disc: mass spread from 0 to R (I = ½MR²). Sphere: mass distributed in 3D (I = ⅖MR²). The further the mass from axis, the larger I.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Colour-coded radial mass distribution with I values ranked: ring > disc > sphere
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorise formulas without understanding — seeing mass distribution visually explains WHY the coefficients differ

simulation_states.label: What is moment of inertia?
simulation_states.description: A point mass m at distance r from axis. I = mr². Rotating it requires torque. Moving mass further from axis → I increases → harder to rotate.
simulation_states.emphasis: I depends on distance from axis, not just mass

simulation_states.label: Ring vs disc
simulation_states.description: Ring and disc of same M, R side by side. Ring: all mass at distance R. Disc: mass distributed from 0 to R. Same torque applied — disc spins faster (lower I).
simulation_states.emphasis: Ring I = MR² > Disc I = ½MR² because ring has all mass at maximum r

simulation_states.label: All shapes compared
simulation_states.description: Ring, disc, solid sphere, hollow sphere shown with I values. Colour shows radial mass distribution. Ranked: ring > hollow sphere > disc > solid sphere.
simulation_states.emphasis: Mass closer to axis → smaller I → easier to spin

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student selects shape, adjusts M and R. Apply same torque — watch angular acceleration α = τ/I. Parallel axis theorem demo: shift axis away from centre and see I increase.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same torque, different I → different α. Shift axis → I increases by Md².

common_misconceptions.belief: Heavier objects always have larger moment of inertia
common_misconceptions.correction: Moment of inertia depends on mass AND its distribution relative to the axis. A light ring can have larger I than a heavy solid sphere if the ring's radius is large enough.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a light ring with larger I than a heavy sphere by adjusting radii

common_misconceptions.belief: The perpendicular axis theorem works for all shapes
common_misconceptions.correction: The perpendicular axis theorem (I_z = I_x + I_y) applies ONLY to planar (flat, 2D) bodies like discs and rings. For 3D bodies like spheres and cylinders, use the parallel axis theorem instead.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show theorem working for disc but NOT for sphere with clear explanation
